text.title: django-models Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for django-models. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: All domain models must inherit from BaseModel. BaseModel provides integer PK (implicit id), a UUID external identifier (uid), created_at, and updated_at.
rule.rationale: Consistent base eliminates boilerplate. Integer PK is used for internal joins; uid is the stable external identifier exposed to APIs and clients.

rule.statement: Supply on_delete= explicitly on every ForeignKey. Use PROTECT for data you must not lose, CASCADE only for owned children, SET_NULL for optional references.
rule.rationale: The default is not safe; omitting on_delete causes a migration error in Django 2.0+ and signals unclear intent in code review.

rule.statement: Add related_name= to every ForeignKey. If the reverse accessor is not needed, use related_name='+'.
rule.rationale: Without related_name, Django generates a default name that clashes when two models share a parent, causing migration errors.

rule.statement: Define all indexes in Meta.indexes with an explicit name following the pattern appname_model_columns_idx. Do not rely on db_index=True for composite indexes.
rule.rationale: Named indexes in Meta.indexes are visible in migrations and reviewable. Pair with EXPLAIN against representative data — don't add indexes speculatively.

rule.statement: Use UniqueConstraint(fields=[...], name="...") instead of deprecated unique_together.
rule.rationale: unique_together is deprecated and will be removed in a future Django version.

rule.statement: Use db_default for database-level defaults (computed at INSERT by the DB). Use default= for application-level defaults (computed by Python). Do not mix them.
rule.rationale: db_default generates different SQL migrations and applies at a different layer. Confusing them produces migrations that fail on older DB versions (requires Postgres 12+ / SQLite 3.38+).

rule.statement: Define all field choices as TextChoices subclasses in a per-app constants.py. Reference them from models, admin, forms, and serializers via the same import.
rule.rationale: Single source of truth prevents drift between the choices used in the model definition and those used in forms/serializers.

rule.statement: Import constants using the module alias pattern: from apps.users import constants as user_constants. Do not do from apps.users.constants import UserType.
rule.rationale: Module alias makes the origin explicit at call sites and prevents ambiguous name collisions when multiple apps have a UserType.

rule.statement: Each app follows the standard structure with models/, services/, tests/, constants.py, and migrations/. Split models into individual files under models/ for apps with more than 2 models.
rule.rationale: Flat models.py becomes unwieldy; splitting into models/user.py, models/order.py keeps each file focused and reduces merge conflicts.
